title: Malignant Hyperthermia Under Volatile Anesthesia
description: During sevoflurane maintenance after succinylcholine, EtCO2 climbs relentlessly with tachycardia and rising temperature. Stop the trigger, hyperventilate on 100% O2, give dantrolene, cool.
learningObjectives: Recognize early MH: rising EtCO2 refractory to ventilation, tachycardia, rigidity
Sequence the MH response: stop trigger, 100% O2 + hyperventilate, dantrolene, cooling
Explain why dantrolene (not cooling alone) treats the hypermetabolism
State the dantrolene dose: 2.5 mg/kg rapid IV, repeated as needed

events.message: Maintenance on sevoflurane after RSI with succinylcholine. Case underway.
EtCO2 keeps climbing despite increasing minute ventilation. The surgeon mentions the patient feels warm.
Masseter feels tight; HR is rising. Temperature is now trending up.

expectedActions.feedback: Trigger agent OFF — the essential first step.
MH is a team emergency — help and the MH cart.

dangerousActions.feedback: EtCO2 above 60 was left unaddressed — in this context that delay costs minutes of hypermetabolism.
Increasing the volatile agent feeds the trigger — the agent must be stopped, not deepened.

debrief.summary: Classic MH: an uncoupled calcium release drives muscle hypermetabolism — EtCO2 rises first and will not clear with ventilation alone; only dantrolene turns off the process.
teachingPoints: A climbing EtCO2 refractory to hyperventilation is the earliest reliable MH sign.
Stop the trigger and run 100% O2 at high fresh gas flow while help arrives.
Dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg rapid IV, repeated to effect — cooling alone controls temperature but not the metabolism.
Each 10-minute delay in dantrolene measurably increases complications.
